text: Fragment text
Ascended cultivators have never truly returned. Sect records only mention rosy clouds receiving them, yet no one has ever left a message from the upper realm.
After one ascension tribulation, a vast face briefly appeared in the sky, as if looking down at offerings through the clouds.

angle: Bone Ash under the Ascension Platform
text: Large amounts of charred bone powder are buried beneath an ancient ascension platform. Examination shows they are not demon beast remains, but scraps of high-realm cultivators' dao bones.
An ancestor who failed to ascend spent years in madness, repeating only: 'Beyond the door is not the immortal realm. It is the space between teeth.'
Some ascension ritual patterns are not structures of 'rising' at all, but resemble sacrificial arrays that package essence blood, soul, and dao insight for delivery.
The 'immortal opportunities' that sometimes descend from the upper realm always lure cultivators into faster breakthroughs, like feed scattered by a keeper to hasten ripening.
The sky sometimes flickers for the briefest instant, and the positions of the stars slip into arrangements that violate celestial patterns.
When high-realm cultivators attempt ascension, it is not that they cannot reach beyond the sky; at a certain height, they strike an unseen wall.
Star charts recorded in old books are utterly unlike tonight's sky, yet ley lines, mountains, rivers, and sect positions still correspond one by one.
In a remote countryside, someone once found remains of a 'visitor from beyond the sky'. They were not flesh, but pieces joined from jade, iron, and clear crystal plates.
The sky inside certain secret realms lags several breaths behind the outer sky, as though two projections were driven by different array disks.
A very few who neared death in enlightenment saw countless fine rune lines behind the sky; sun, moon, and stars were only points of light upon those lines.
The laws of the world sometimes twist strangely; the same spirit herb can show entirely opposite medicinal properties on different days.
True Persons above the Tongxuan realm often share the same nightmare: a pale great eye deep in the firmament, laughing and crying without end.
For the past century, tribulation lightning has gone wrong: the virtuous are sometimes blasted to soul-death, while villains are occasionally refined into sacred bodies.
Ancient documents of sacrifice to Heaven record responses that were brief and solemn; modern planchette messages are full of repetition, errors, and meaningless babble.
Someone used a karmic secret art to trace an enemy, only for the clue to loop back before their own birth, as if karmic threads had been tied in random knots.
An ancient stele records: 'Heaven is not dead, not evil, but ill. The ill one creates worlds in dream and destroys worlds upon waking.'
Cultivators of unknown origin sometimes appear suddenly in the world. They possess complete identities, yet no one remembers their growth.
Cultivators often discover their memories subtly altered: yesterday's technique, a loved one's name, or sect history has changed in small ways.
Some dying people claim they saw heaven and earth pause: everyone froze, and countless tiny words scrolled through the air.
When diviners infer fate, they sometimes receive non-hexagram words such as 'null', 'retry', 'seed', or 'invalid state'.
A few spirit treasures contain no array core, only a strange crystal board covered in tiny squares and metallic circuits.
When many major events occur in the same month, the world briefly stutters: birds stop in the air and rivers advance frame by frame.
During midnight breathing practice, cultivators sometimes hear strangers crying, laughing, and shouting at death as the sounds sweep through their meridians.
Ancient battlefields have the richest spiritual qi, yet contain no spirit veins, only the bones of countless mortals and cultivators.
Some who break through after seclusion suddenly know dialects, crafts, or old memories they never learned.
When high-purity spirit stones are refined, faint human silhouettes appear inside, like many faces compressed into one crystal.
Once a haunted forbidden land is thoroughly purified, the surrounding qi does not become clean; it rapidly dries up instead.
An ancient soul cultivator left these words: 'Do not ask whence qi comes. Every breath you take is the second death of multitudes.'
Deep beneath many sects lie white mineral layers shaped like bones, indestructible and faintly warm on moonlit nights.
When ley-line maps are connected, they do not resemble mountains and rivers, but the meridians and blood vessels of some immense creature.
Some spirit springs occasionally redden and carry a faint smell of blood, yet greatly increase cultivation.
Old books say, 'Immortal mountains have veins, and veins have heartbeats,' but later commentaries always explain heartbeat as qi tides.
Every few centuries, a sect's great array must 'seal the vein'; otherwise dull breathing sounds rise from underground.
A tomb robber dug a vast eye socket inside a mountain; the remaining pupil membrane reflected the locations of all present sects.
Reborn people often have memories of a past life, but lack the soul aura that life should have, like a book copied anew.
Underworld ruins contain no reincarnation passage, only blank jade slips carved with names, births, temperaments, and karmas.
Soul summoning can call a 'past-life shadow', but if asked about something not recorded, the shadow falls into repeated answers.
A cultivator met three people claiming the same previous life; their memories differed and lacked pieces, yet all passed heavenly verification.
An ancient nether cultivator wrote: 'Reincarnation is not a river, but a printing block; the soul does not return, the characters are carved again.'
A newborn once spoke a past life's final words, while at the same moment that past soul was still wailing inside its old tomb.
The oldest yao sacrificial texts are not beast speech, but a damaged ancient human script.
Deep in some yao bloodlines appear human sect marks, even able to open long-lost human grottoes.
Human infants sometimes revert into yao, while yao young are sometimes born with human meridian structures.
In some yao ancestral lands, murals show ancestors with no beast form, dressed as ancient humans beneath a cracking sky.
Before the 'Era of Yao Chaos', old books record no yao, only vague terms such as 'great migration', 'body calamity', and 'bloodline refuge'.
Inside a yao saint's remains lay a jade slip whose first line read: 'We were once called human; we merely survived the day Heaven changed its skin.'
Early ancient methods had no fixed realms, only continuous descriptions such as 'qi full', 'spirit bright', 'body transformed', and 'dao joined'.
Modern cultivators must face bottlenecks when breaking through, but wild spirit beasts rarely meet similar barriers at the same level of power.
Some incomplete ancient arts bypass realms to increase power, but tribulation lightning targets them abnormally, as if a prohibition were violated.
Golden Cores and Nascent Souls are highly similar across different methods, as if forcibly templated by the same rule set.
An old stele says: 'The stairway is not for climbing to Heaven, but for measuring livestock.'
A cultivator briefly saw through the realm lock and discovered their cultivation was not growing, but being released through permit seals one by one.
On full-moon nights, the moon in some water reflections blinks first, and only afterward does a faint shadow appear on the real moon.
Moonlight cultivation methods advance quickly, but those who master them often grow a silver pupil mark between the brows.
Those who stare at the moon too long dream they are placed on a table in endless darkness and read by a vast gaze.
Ancient astronomical charts contain no moon, only a 'Night Eye' sealed by red threads.
Some yao beasts kneel together at full moon, not to worship it, but to avoid the moonlight's direct gaze.
A fragment from beyond the sky bears a warning: 'Do not let it remember your true name.'
Rules inside secret realms often differ from the outside: time, gravity, life and death, and qi attributes all seem locally out of control.
Mountains and rivers near secret-realm entrances gradually deform: trees grow beast bones, and stones develop pulses.
Many treasures brought back from secret realms trigger new small abnormal zones in the outside world.
Ancient physician cultivators likened the world to a giant and called secret realms 'nodules', 'malignant sores', and 'rotting grotto-heavens'.
Deep in some secret realms there is no treasury, only a vast ulcerated world membrane slowly leaking spiritual qi.
The more cultivators exploit secret realms, the more frequent the world's anomalies become, as if lesions are repeatedly cut open and infected.
Temple statues sometimes shed black corpse oil, which believers mistake for divine signs.
A single deity's responses to prayer repeat mechanically; no matter the era, language, or disaster, the blessing hardly changes.
A giant severed finger was found deep in an ancient battlefield, bearing divine patterns identical to those of a current righteous god.
Where incense offerings flourish, statues look more alive, but temple keepers more often dream of rotting heavenly palaces.
Ancient ritual texts do not say 'invite the god to descend', but 'wake the remains for a moment'.

angle: Corpse Mountain in the Divine Realm
text: After ascending to a divine kingdom, one devout believer saw only endless corpse mountains, each body still listening to prayers.
